description: The quintessential summer flower, Russian Giant sunflowers are famous for their incredible height and massive, golden-yellow heads. They are excellent for pollinators and provide a wonderful spectacle in any garden or large container. Children love growing them to see how high they can go!

characteristics.waterNeeds: high and THEN info: High — 'water hogs' during peak growth
characteristics.containerSize: 15L+ pot for giant varieties (or large troughs for dwarfs)

care.sowIndoors: Sow seeds 1.5cm deep in individual pots in April. Keep on a sunny windowsill at 18–21°C. They grow fast, so may need potting up before planting out.
care.sowOutdoors: Sow directly in late May once frost has passed. Clear the area of weeds and protect from slugs immediately.
care.hardenOff: Gradually acclimatise to outdoor conditions over 7–10 days in May.
care.plantOut: Space giant varieties at least 45cm apart. Provide a sturdy stake (bamboo cane) at planting time to support the future height.
care.watering: Water daily in hot weather. Deep watering encourages a strong taproot.
care.feeding: Feed weekly with a balanced liquid fertiliser until flowering, then switch to a high-potash (tomato) feed.
care.harvest: Cut flowers for vases when the petals start to unfurl. To save seeds, leave the head on the plant until it turns brown and the back feels papery.
care.overwintering: Annual — leave dead heads for the birds in autumn, then compost the stalks.

propagation.seedDescription: Large, teardrop-shaped seeds with a striped or solid grey/black shell.

deadheading.notes: Deadheading the main bloom won't usually produce more flowers on giant single-stem varieties, but it can encourage side shoots on multi-branching types.

seedSaving.method: Cover the ripening head with mesh to protect from birds. Once the back turns brown and the seeds are plump, cut the head and hang it in a dry place to finish drying.
seedSaving.seedPodDescription: Large central disc packed with hundreds of individual seeds.

commonProblems: Slugs and snails (very dangerous to seedlings)
funFact: Sunflowers are 'phytoremediators'—they were planted at Chernobyl to help extract radioactive toxins from the soil!
